messages.body: Let me look at a few more key files to get a complete picture.
Now let me check a few more files to see validators and the Startup/DI configuration.

Based on my thorough exploration of the codebase, here's where and how SOLID principles are applied in this project:

---

### S — Single Responsibility Principle

Each project/layer has a focused responsibility:
- **Core** (`SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Core`) — Domain models, interfaces, validators. No infrastructure concerns.
- **Infrastructure** (`SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Infrastructure.EntityFramework`) — Data access only via [ModelingRepository.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Infrastructure.EntityFramework\ModelingRepository.cs) and [ModelingRepositoryBase.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Infrastructure.EntityFramework\ModelingRepositoryBase.cs).
- **Grpc** (`SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc`) — API/transport layer. The service implementation is split across partial classes by domain area: [ModelingServiceImpl.Calculations.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\ModelingServiceImpl.Calculations.cs), [ModelingServiceImpl.Layouts.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\ModelingServiceImpl.Layouts.cs), [ModelingServiceImpl.Tests.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\ModelingServiceImpl.Tests.cs), etc.
- **Validators** are each in their own class per entity (e.g., [ModelValidator.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Core\Validators\ComplianceModel\ModelValidator.cs), `BlockValidator`, `GroupValidator`), one validator per model type.

---

### O — Open/Closed Principle

- **FluentValidation** validators extend `IdentityValidator<T>` (from the common library), adding entity-specific rules without modifying the base. New validators for new entities can be added without changing existing ones.
- **AutoMapper profiles** — [GrpcToModelAutoMapperProfile.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\GrpcToModelAutoMapperProfile.cs) extends mapping behavior additively. The `AllowAdditiveTypeMapCreation = true` setting in [Startup.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\Internal\Startup.cs#L89) explicitly supports this.
- **Cache data providers** — The caching system in [ServiceCollectionExtensions.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Shared\ServiceCollectionExtensions.cs) selects between `WassupsEventMapDataProvider`, `WassupsBasedMapDataProvider`, or `ServiceBasedMapDataProvider` based on configuration, all implementing `IObjectMapDataProvider<T>`.

---

### L — Liskov Substitution Principle

- [ModelElement.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Core\Model\ModelElement.cs) is an abstract base class extending `Identity`. All domain entities (Test, Block, Group, Waterfall, etc.) inherit from it and can be used wherever `ModelElement` or `Identity` is expected.
- [ModelingRepositoryBase.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Infrastructure.EntityFramework\ModelingRepositoryBase.cs) provides base repository behavior, and [ModelingRepository.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Infrastructure.EntityFramework\ModelingRepository.cs) extends it — the base class's `RepoCallAsync` and `InitializeAsync` work correctly regardless of which subclass is used.

---

### I — Interface Segregation Principle

- [IModelingRepository.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Core\IModelingRepository.cs) extends both `IRepositoryItems` and `IRepositoryItemsClassic`, composing two separate interfaces rather than one monolithic one.
- Small, focused interfaces for model behaviors: [IApplicabilityExpressionItem](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Core\Model\IApplicabilityExpressionItem.cs), `IFormulaExpressionItem`, and `ICriteriaExpressionItem`. Entities implement only the expression interfaces they need (e.g., eligibility criteria implements both `IApplicabilityExpressionItem` and `ICriteriaExpressionItem`, while recovery rules only implement `IApplicabilityExpressionItem`).
- [IModelingDbContext.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Shared\IModelingDbContext.cs) exposes only the DbSets needed by the shared layer, not the full DbContext surface.

---

### D — Dependency Inversion Principle

This is the most prominently applied principle:

- **The Core layer defines [IModelingRepository](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Core\IModelingRepository.cs)** (abstraction), and the Infrastructure layer provides [ModelingRepository](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Infrastructure.EntityFramework\ModelingRepository.cs) (implementation). The Grpc service layer depends only on the interface.
- **Constructor injection everywhere** — [ModelingServiceImpl](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\ModelingServiceImpl.cs#L96) receives `IModelingRepository`, `ITracer`, `IAmbientStateManager`, `IOidcResourceAuthorizer`, `IDatabaseRouter`, etc. — all abstractions.
- **DI wiring in [Startup.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\Internal\Startup.cs#L107)** registers `ModelingRepository` as a singleton and maps it to `IModelingRepository`:
  ```csharp
  .AddSingleton<ModelingRepository>()
  .AddSingleton<IModelingRepository>(svcProvider => svcProvider.GetRequiredService<ModelingRepository>())
  ```
- **Validators are resolved via DI** using `_serviceProvider.GetRequiredService<IValidator<U>>()` in the service layer, decoupling validation logic from the API layer.
- **Cache abstractions** (`IObjectMap<T>`, `IObjectLookup<T>`, `IObjectMapDataProvider<T>`) are injected into the repository, allowing different caching strategies to be swapped without changing repository code.

---

### Areas where SOLID could be improved

- **IModelingRepository** is quite large (~100+ methods). It could benefit from being split into smaller, domain-specific interfaces (e.g., `IComplianceModelRepository`, `ILayoutRepository`) — better ISP adherence.
- **ModelingServiceImpl**, even though split into partial classes, is still a single class with many responsibilities. Each domain area could potentially be its own gRPC service or handler.
How would you explain to your interviewer how gRPC framework is used in this project and what is it and why you chose it above REST?

Great question — here's how I'd structure that answer for an interview, grounded in what this project actually does:

---

### What is gRPC?

gRPC is a high-performance, open-source RPC (Remote Procedure Call) framework originally developed by Google. It uses **Protocol Buffers (protobuf)** as its interface definition language and serialization format, and runs over **HTTP/2**. You define your service contract in `.proto` files, and the framework generates strongly-typed client and server code for you.

---

### How gRPC is used in this project

This is a **compliance modeling microservice** in a larger distributed system (the Solvas Fabric platform). Here's how gRPC fits in:

1. **Service definition via proto files** — The [GrpcApi](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.GrpcApi) project contains the `.proto` files under the `api/` folder that define the service contract: all request/response messages and RPC methods (CRUD for compliance models, tests, waterfalls, layouts, sequences, etc.).

2. **Server implementation** — [ModelingServiceImpl](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\ModelingServiceImpl.cs) extends the auto-generated `ModelingService.ModelingServiceBase` class. Each RPC method (like `GetComplianceModel`, `ListTests`, `CreateWaterfall`) is an override of a generated base method. The implementation is split across partial classes by domain area to keep things organized.

3. **Service registration** — In [Startup.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\Internal\Startup.cs), the service is registered with:
   ```csharp
   .AddHostApi<ModelingServiceImpl>(ModelingService.BindService, ModelingService.Descriptor)
   ```

4. **Inter-service communication** — The project also acts as a gRPC *client* to other microservices. You can see client registrations for DomainData, Organizations, Wassups, and even itself (for the expression rule cache):
   ```csharp
   .AddApiClient<DomainDataService.DomainDataServiceClient>()
   .AddApiClient<OrganizationsService.OrganizationsServiceClient>()
   .AddApiClient<ModelingService.ModelingServiceClient>()
   ```

5. **Interceptors for cross-cutting concerns** — gRPC interceptors handle authorization (`ResourceNameAuthorizationInterceptor`), request validation (`ValidateRequestInterceptor`), and scope injection (`InjectGenericScopeInterceptor`) — similar to middleware in REST but native to the gRPC pipeline.

6. **AutoMapper for DTO translation** — Protobuf-generated message types (the gRPC DTOs) are mapped to/from domain models using AutoMapper via [GrpcToModelAutoMapperProfile](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\GrpcToModelAutoMapperProfile.cs), keeping a clean separation between the transport and domain layers.

---

### Why gRPC over REST?

For this project specifically, I'd highlight these reasons:

- **Service-to-service communication in a microservices architecture** — This isn't a public-facing API consumed by browsers. It's backend services talking to each other. gRPC's binary protobuf serialization is significantly faster and more compact than JSON, which matters when you have dozens of services exchanging data constantly.

- **Strongly-typed contracts** — Proto files act as a single source of truth. When the compliance modeling service changes its API, the generated client code in consuming services breaks at compile time, not at runtime. In a complex domain like compliance modeling with 50+ entity types, this catches integration issues early.

- **HTTP/2 benefits** — Multiplexing (multiple requests over a single connection), header compression, and bidirectional streaming. This project uses server streaming for some operations and the persistent connections reduce latency between services.

- **Code generation** — The [BuildProtoFiles](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.GrpcApi\BuildProtoFiles) setup auto-generates C# client/server stubs, request/response classes, and serialization code. This eliminates an entire class of bugs around manual HTTP client construction, URL building, and JSON deserialization that you'd deal with in REST.

- **First-class support for the patterns already in use** — The Solvas Fabric platform standardizes on gRPC across all services (you can see the shared `SolvasFabric.Grpc.Hosting` and `SolvasFabric.Grpc.Client` libraries). Using REST for one service would mean maintaining a separate set of infrastructure (routing, middleware, serialization) that doesn't align with the rest of the platform.

---

### When would I still use REST?

I'd mention that REST is still the better choice for public APIs, browser clients, or when you need human-readable payloads for debugging. In fact, this project includes [http-testing](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\http-testing) files that suggest there may be an HTTP gateway or transcoding layer sitting in front of the gRPC services for testing or external access — which is a common hybrid pattern.
What caching strategy is used here?
Let me dig deeper into the caching implementation to give you a thorough answer.
Now let me check how the caches are used in the repository and how they're initialized.

Here's a comprehensive breakdown of the caching strategy used in this project:

---

### Overview: Multi-layered, event-driven in-memory caching

The project uses **in-memory object caches** that are kept in sync across service instances using an **event-driven invalidation/update mechanism** called "Wassups" (the platform's internal event streaming system, similar to a CDC/event bus).

---

### What gets cached

There are **5 distinct caches**, each for a different type of frequently-accessed, cross-service data. You can see them all initialized in [Startup.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\Internal\Startup.cs):

1. **Reference Data (Lookup Items)** — `IObjectLookup<LookupItem>` — Lookup codes like rating agencies, calculation methods, etc. Keyed by lookup type.
2. **Data Dictionary Fields** — `IObjectMap<Field>` — Field definitions from the domain data service. Used heavily when resolving field names/IDs in layouts, hypothetical field changes, etc.
3. **Function Definitions** — `IObjectMap<FunctionDefinition>` — Reusable function definitions from domain data.
4. **Expression Rules** — `IObjectMap<Rule>` — The compliance modeling service's own expression rules. This is a self-referencing cache (the service caches its own data).
5. **Portfolios** — `IObjectMap<Portfolio>` — Portfolio entities from the organizations service.

---

### Two cache abstractions

- **`IObjectLookup<T>`** — A key-to-list store (one lookup type → many lookup items). Used for reference data.
- **`IObjectMap<T>`** — A key-to-object store (one ID → one entity). Used for fields, rules, portfolios, function definitions.

Both are **scoped by owner** (tenant). Every cache operation begins with `cache.BeginScope(ownerId, true)` to isolate tenant data, as seen throughout [ModelingRepository.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Infrastructure.EntityFramework\ModelingRepository.cs).

---

### Three data provider strategies (configurable)

The cache population strategy is **configurable per environment** via app settings, as implemented in [ServiceCollectionExtensions.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Shared\ServiceCollectionExtensions.cs):

1. **Wassups Events** (`WassupsEventMapDataProvider`) — When `useWassupsEvents: true`. The cache is populated and kept up-to-date by consuming real-time change events from the Wassups event stream. [WassupsModelingCacheConsumerHandler](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Shared\WassupsModelingCacheConsumerHandler.cs) processes add/update/delete events and applies them directly to the in-memory cache.

2. **Wassups Tables** (`WassupsBasedMapDataProvider`) — When `useWassupsTables: true`. Instead of consuming events, it reads from local Wassup tables (replicated data stored in the service's own database via [WassupModelingConsumerHandler](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Shared\WassupModelingConsumerHandler.cs)). This is essentially a **local read replica** — the Wassup consumer writes entity events into local EF tables, and the cache reads from those tables.

3. **Service-based** (`ServiceBasedMapDataProvider`) — The fallback. It calls the service's own gRPC API to fetch data, as seen in [ServiceBasedMapDataProvider.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Shared\CacheDataProvider\ServiceBasedMapDataProvider.cs). For example, expression rules are fetched via `ListModelExpressionRulesAsync`.

---

### Cache lifecycle

1. **Startup** — Cache monitors are started in [Startup.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc\Internal\Startup.cs) after the Wassups consumer host starts:
   ```csharp
   wassupsConsumerHost.StartAsync()
   lookupItemsCacheMonitor.StartAsync(...)
   dataDictionaryFieldsCacheMonitor.StartAsync(...)
   // etc.
   ```
   Each `IObjectMapMonitoringHost<T>` periodically rebuilds the cache at a configurable interval (default 1 hour, overridable via `memoryCache:*:refreshIntervalInSeconds`).

2. **Runtime reads** — The repository uses a **cache-first with fallback** pattern. For example, in [ModelingRepository.cs](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Infrastructure.EntityFramework\ModelingRepository.cs), the expression rule cache:
   ```csharp
   public async Task<Rule> GetModelExpressionRuleByIdCachedAsync(...)
   {
     try {
       using (_expRuleCache.BeginScope(ownerId, true))
         return await _expRuleCache.GetOrAddAsync(ruleId, cancellationToken);
     }
     catch (GenericMapException) {
       // fallback to non-cached version if cache is empty
       return await GetModelExpressionRuleByIdAsync(ownerId, ruleId, cancellationToken);
     }
   }
   ```

3. **Readiness checks** — Before using a cache, the repository ensures it's populated using a **backoff/retry pattern**:
   ```csharp
   private Task EnsureObjectMapIsPopulated<T>(string ownerId, IObjectMap<T> genericMap, ...)
     => Backoff.RunAsync(
       action: (_) => { genericMap.BeginScope(ownerId, true); return Task.CompletedTask; },
       errorHandler: (exception, nextDuration, _) => {
         if (exception is GenericMapException) { /* retry */ }
       });
   ```
   This ensures the service waits for caches to be warm before serving requests.

4. **Explicit refresh** — After mutations, the repository explicitly rebuilds relevant caches. For example, after creating/updating an expression rule:
   ```csharp
   await RefreshModelExpressionRulesCache(ownerId, cancellationToken);
   ```

5. **Shutdown** — All cache monitors are gracefully stopped.

---

### Change tracking for smart rebuilds

The `ServiceBasedMapDataProvider` implements `IObjectMapChangeTrackingDataProvider<T>`, which uses a `ChangeTracker` (record count + latest ETag/version) to detect whether the cache needs rebuilding. The `GetModelExpressionRulesStatistics` endpoint returns the count and max row version from the database, so the cache monitor can skip a full rebuild if nothing changed.

---

### Summary

| Aspect | Strategy |
|---|---|
| Cache type | In-memory, per-tenant scoped |
| Invalidation | Event-driven (Wassups) + periodic polling + explicit refresh after writes |
| Population | 3 configurable strategies (events, local tables, gRPC self-call) |
| Fallback | Graceful degradation to direct DB/service calls if cache is empty |
| Warm-up | Backoff/retry pattern blocks requests until caches are ready |
| Data cached | Cross-service reference data (fields, lookups, portfolios, functions) + own expression rules |

using SolvasAm.Common;
using SolvasAm.Common.Models;
using SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Core.Application.Sequence;
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

namespace SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Grpc
{
  /// <summary>
  /// AutoMapper profile to configure mapping core model classes to gRPC DTO classes.
  /// </summary>
  public class GrpcToModelAutoMapperProfile : ModelAutoMapperProfile
  {
    public GrpcToModelAutoMapperProfile()
    {
      var types = new Dictionary<Type, Type>
      {
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.ComplianceModel), typeof(Core.Model.ComplianceModel.Model) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.ComplianceModelHistory), typeof(Core.Model.ComplianceModel.History) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.Sequence), typeof(Core.Model.Sequence.Sequence) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.SequenceStep), typeof(Core.Model.Sequence.Step) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.VerifyStep), typeof(VerifyStep) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.HypotheticalFieldChangeScenario), typeof(Core.Model.HypotheticalFieldChange.Scenario) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.HypotheticalFieldChangeRule), typeof(Core.Model.HypotheticalFieldChange.Rule) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.CustomStep), typeof(Core.Model.Sequence.Custom) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.AvailableStep), typeof(Core.Model.Sequence.Component) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.ModelIdPair), typeof(Core.Model.ModelIdPair) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.Template), typeof(Core.Model.Template.Template) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.TemplateParameter), typeof(Core.Model.Template.Parameter) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.TemplateParameterPurpose), typeof(Core.Model.Template.ParameterPurpose) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.TemplateParameterDataType), typeof(Core.Model.Template.ParameterDataType) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.TemplateParameterValueType), typeof(Core.Model.Template.ParameterValueType) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.TemplateCustomSuccessCriteria), typeof(Core.Model.Template.CustomSuccessCriteria) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.TemplatePortfolio), typeof(Core.Model.Template.TemplatePortfolio) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.EligibilityCriteria), typeof(Core.Model.Template.EligibilityCriteria) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.Layout), typeof(Core.Model.Layout.Layout) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.LayoutColumn), typeof(Core.Model.Layout.Column) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.ImportLayout), typeof(Core.Model.ImportLayout.Layout) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.ImportColumn), typeof(Core.Model.ImportLayout.ImportColumn) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.StaticColumn), typeof(Core.Model.ImportLayout.StaticColumn) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.ImportColumnValueMap), typeof(Core.Model.ImportLayout.ValueMap) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.ImportColumnData), typeof(Core.Model.ImportLayout.Data) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.RecoveryScenario), typeof(Core.Model.RecoveryRate.Scenario) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.RecoveryRule), typeof(Core.Model.RecoveryRate.Rule) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.RatingScenario), typeof(Core.Model.Rating.Scenario) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.RatingRule), typeof(Core.Model.Rating.Rule) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.PBRuleGroup), typeof(Core.Model.PrincipalBalance.Group) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.PBRule), typeof(Core.Model.PrincipalBalance.Rule) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.CalculationGroup), typeof(Core.Model.Calculation.Group) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.CalculationBlock), typeof(Core.Model.Calculation.Block) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.CalculationBlockParameter), typeof(Core.Model.Calculation.Parameter) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.EligibilityCriteriaTest), typeof(Core.Model.Test.EligibilityCriteria) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.Test), typeof(Core.Model.Test.Test) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.TestParameter), typeof(Core.Model.Test.Parameter) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.HistoricalValue), typeof(Core.Model.HistoricalValue) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.TestParameterHistoricalValue), typeof(Core.Model.Test.ParameterHistoricalValue) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.TestCustomSuccessCriteria), typeof(Core.Model.Test.CustomSuccessCriteria) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.ApplicabilityCriteria), typeof(Core.Model.ApplicabilityCriteria) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.PaymentGroup), typeof(Core.Model.PaymentGroup.Group) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.PaymentGroupStep), typeof(Core.Model.PaymentGroup.Step) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.PaymentGroupStepParameter), typeof(Core.Model.PaymentGroup.Parameter) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.Waterfall), typeof(Core.Model.PriorityPayment.Waterfall) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.WaterfallStepParameter), typeof(Core.Model.PriorityPayment.Parameter) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.Field), typeof(Core.Application.FieldsUsed.Field) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.FieldDetail), typeof(Core.Application.FieldsUsed.Detail) },
        { typeof(Core.Model.Calculation.Parameter), typeof(Core.Model.PriorityPayment.Parameter) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.ReinvestmentPayment), typeof(Core.Model.Setting.ReinvestmentPayment) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.ReinvestmentRate), typeof(Core.Model.Setting.ReinvestmentRate) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.ReinvestmentPaymentSchedule), typeof(Core.Application.Setting.ReinvestmentPaymentSchedule) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.OverCollateralization), typeof(Core.Model.Setting.OverCollateralization) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.InterestCoverage), typeof(Core.Model.Setting.InterestCoverage) },
        { typeof(GrpcApi.V1.EffectiveSpread), typeof(Core.Model.Setting.EffectiveSpread) }
      };

      foreach (var pair in types)
      {
        var grpcType = pair.Key;
        var modelType = pair.Value;

        var map = CreateMap(modelType, grpcType);// Map domain model TSource to gRPC DTO TDestination and vice versa.
        if (grpcType.GetMember("ActionMasks").Length > 0 && typeof(IActionMask).IsAssignableFrom(modelType))
        {
          map.ForMember("ActionMasks", opt => opt.MapFrom(src =>
              ((IActionMask)src).ActionMasks == null ? null : ((IActionMask)src).ActionMasks.Select(l => new SolvasAm.Common.GrpcApi.V1.CodePair { Code = l.Key, Description = l.Value })
          ));
        }
        map.ReverseMap();

        if (grpcType.GetMember("Name").Length > 0)
        {
          map.ForMember("Name", opt => opt.Ignore());
        }
        if (grpcType.GetMember("ActionMasks").Length > 0)
        {
          map.ForMember("ActionMasks", opt => opt.Ignore());
        }

        CreateMap(modelType, modelType); //to facilitate deep copy.

      }

      CreateMap<Core.Model.Calculation.Parameter, Core.Model.PaymentGroup.Parameter>()
       .ForMember(p => p.StepIdClassic, opt => opt.Ignore())
       .ForMember(p => p.StepId, opt => opt.Ignore())
       .ForMember(p => p.StepCode, opt => opt.Ignore())
       .ReverseMap();

      //ComplianceModel
      CreateMap<GrpcApi.V1.ComplianceModel, Core.Model.ComplianceModel.Model>()
         .ForMember(dest => dest.PortfolioId, opt => opt.Ignore())
         .ForMember(dest => dest.PortfolioIdClassic, opt => opt.MapFrom(src => src.PortfolioId))
         .ForMember(dest => dest.SequenceId, opt => opt.Ignore())
         .ForMember(dest => dest.SequenceIdClassic, opt => opt.MapFrom(src => src.SequenceId))
         .ForMember(dest => dest.ActionMasks, opt => opt.Ignore())
         .ForMember(dest => dest.CopySourceIdClassic, opt => opt.MapFrom(src => src.CopySourceId))
         .ForMember(dest => dest.CopySourceId, opt => opt.Ignore())
         .ReverseMap()
         .ForMember(dest => dest.PortfolioId, opt => opt.MapFrom(src => src.PortfolioIdClassic))
         .ForMember(dest => dest.ActionMasks, opt => opt.MapFrom(src => src.ActionMasks == null ? null : src.ActionMasks.Select(l => new SolvasAm.Common.GrpcApi.V1.CodePair { Code = l.Key, Description = l.Value })))
         .ForMember(dest => dest.CopySourceId, opt => opt.MapFrom(src => src.CopySourceIdClassic));

      //ComplianceModel.History

messages.body: Add the `Ddx` regex validation to reject `<%...%>` server tags:

input.explanation: Add regex validation on Ddx field to reject ASP-style server tags in ModelExpressionValidator.

messages.body:
This adds a `Must` rule on `Ddx` that rejects any value matching `<%...%>`. Since this validator only applies to `ModelExpression` (used for `applicability_expression` / `formula_expression` on entities like RatingRule, RecoveryRule, etc.), it won't affect `Expression.Rule` used by CreateModelExpressionRule/UpdateModelExpressionRule — that type has its own separate [RuleValidator](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-common\src\SolvasAm.Common\Validators\Application\Expression\RuleValidator.cs).

As noted in my previous analysis, you'll also need to ensure the domain validators in the compliance-modeling project actually invoke this child validator via `.SetValidator(new ModelExpressionValidator())` on their `ApplicabilityExpression` properties — currently those rules are empty (e.g., `RuleFor(r => r.ApplicabilityExpression);` with no validation attached).
but how will it allow for CreateModelExpressionRule and UpdateModelExpressionRule

You're right to question that. Let me re-examine the flow more carefully.

The key question is: does `CreateModelExpressionRule` / `UpdateModelExpressionRule` also end up validating a `ModelExpression` object through this validator?

Let me check how the Expression Rule's `Ddx` flows through validation.

Now I can confirm: `CreateModelExpressionRule` and `UpdateModelExpressionRule` validate against `IValidator<SolvasAm.Common.Model.Expression.Rule>` — which is the [RuleValidator](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-common\src\SolvasAm.Common\Validators\Application\Expression\RuleValidator.cs). That type (`Expression.Rule`) is a completely **different class** from `Expression.ModelExpression`. The `ModelExpressionValidator` only validates `ModelExpression` objects.

So the change I already made is correct — **it naturally won't affect `CreateModelExpressionRule` / `UpdateModelExpressionRule`** because those endpoints validate `Expression.Rule`, not `Expression.ModelExpression`.

To summarize:
- `CreateModelExpressionRule` / `UpdateModelExpressionRule` → validates `SolvasAm.Common.Model.Expression.Rule` → uses `RuleValidator` → **no `<%...%>` check** ✅
- All other entities with `applicability_expression` (RatingRule, RecoveryRule, PBRule, etc.) → their `ApplicabilityExpression` property is of type `ModelExpression` → uses `ModelExpressionValidator` → **`<%...%>` is rejected** ✅

The `ModelExpressionValidator` change I made is already the right and complete solution. No exclusion logic needed.
ohh

Yeah — the types are completely separate so it just works. The `Expression.Rule` model (used by Create/UpdateModelExpressionRule) and `Expression.ModelExpression` (used for `applicability_expression` on other entities) each have their own validator, so the regex check on `ModelExpressionValidator` only applies where you want it blocked.

The `ModelExpressionValidator` only runs when the model is validated via `MapToModelAndValidate` — but looking at the Rating Rule flow in `CreateRatingRule`/`UpdateRatingRule`, the `ApplicabilityExpression` property on the model (`Rating.Rule`) is of type `ModelExpression`. However, FluentValidation **does not automatically invoke child validators** for nested complex properties unless explicitly wired up.

Looking at the [Rating.RuleValidator](c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-compliance-modeling\src\SolvasAmCompliance.Modeling.Core\Validators\Rating\RuleValidator.cs#L48):

```csharp
RuleFor(r => r.ApplicabilityExpression);  // no validation logic attached
```

This line does nothing — it doesn't call `.SetValidator(...)`. So the `ModelExpressionValidator` is **never invoked** for nested `ApplicabilityExpression` properties.

You need to wire it up explicitly. In each domain validator that has `ApplicabilityExpression`, change:

```csharp
RuleFor(r => r.ApplicabilityExpression);
```

to:

```csharp
RuleFor(r => r.ApplicabilityExpression)
  .SetValidator(new ModelExpressionValidator())
  .When(r => r.ApplicabilityExpression != null);
```

Why would  var filter = "hydrate =\"Lite\""; throw error The filter provided {FilterExpression} could not be compiled into a delegate for cache filtering. Error: {Exception}. Fetching unfiltered...

in after we RefreshModelExpressionRulesCache. It is working fine in my local but not in QA environment which is hosted

The issue is with the filter string `hydrate ="Lite"` being passed to `_filterTranslator.Parse<Rule>(filter)`. This method tries to compile the filter string into a LINQ expression/delegate that can be used to filter `Rule` objects in the cache.

The problem is likely that `hydrate` is not a property on the `Rule` class. The `_filterTranslator.Parse<Rule>(filter)` method attempts to build an expression tree mapping filter keys to properties of `T` (in this case `Rule`). Since `Rule` doesn't have a `hydrate` property, it fails to compile.

**Why it works locally but not in QA:**

The key is in the flow after `RefreshModelExpressionRulesCache`:

1. After the cache refresh, `GetModelExpressionRuleByIdAsync` is called with an ID filter (e.g., `id ="123"`)
2. That calls `GetModelExpressionRulesAsync` with `tryCache = false`, bypassing the cache entirely — so no filter compilation happens locally

But in QA, something else is likely calling `GetModelExpressionRulesAsync` with `filter = "hydrate =\"Lite\""` and `tryCache = true` (the default). This triggers the filter compilation path. The `hydrate` parameter is typically a query-level hint (controlling serialization depth), not a domain property on `Rule` — so the expression translator can't map it.

**The fix:** The `hydrate` filter key should be stripped out before attempting to compile it as a predicate against `Rule`. Or the caller should not pass `hydrate` as part of the filter string when it's meant as a serialization directive, not a data filter.

Now I can see the full picture. Let me look at line 1997 specifically:

At line 1997, the full `request.Filter` (which is `hydrate ="Lite"`) is passed directly to `_repository.GetModelExpressionRulesAsync`. Then inside that method (in your active file), when `tryCache = true` (which is the case for non-caching requests), it tries to compile the filter `hydrate ="Lite"` into a `Func<Rule, bool>` predicate.

But wait — at line 1983-1986, when the request comes from the `ServiceBasedSnapshotCacheDataProvider`, it sets the `caching_request` header to `true`, which means `isCachingRequest = true`, and at line 1997 `!isCachingRequest` = `false`, so `tryCache = false`. This means the cache path (and the filter compilation) is **skipped** for caching requests.

So the question is: **who is calling `ListModelExpressionRules` with `hydrate ="Lite"` but WITHOUT the `caching_request` header?**

Let me check the `ServiceBasedMapDataProvider`:

Now I have the full picture. Here's the root cause:

**The `ServiceBasedMapDataProvider` (the deprecated one) calls `ListModelExpressionRulesAsync` with `filter = "hydrate =\"Lite\""` but does NOT set the `caching_request` header.**

Look at the difference:
- `ServiceBasedSnapshotCacheDataProvider` (line 39): sends `headers: new() { { "caching_request", bool.TrueString }}` → `tryCache = false` → filter compilation is skipped ✅
- `ServiceBasedMapDataProvider` (line 47): sends NO headers → `tryCache = true` (default) → filter compilation is attempted on `hydrate ="Lite"` → **FAILS** because `Rule` has no `hydrate` property ❌

**Why it works locally but not in QA:**

Locally, the snapshot cache (`_expRuleCache`) is likely not initialized yet (or you're hitting the `SnapshotCacheException` fallback), so the code never reaches the `_filterTranslator.Parse<Rule>(filter).Compile()` line. In QA, the cache IS initialized, so the code enters the cache path and tries to compile the filter.

However, looking more carefully at the code flow — the error is logged as `Info` level and the code continues with `predicate = null` (fetching unfiltered from cache). So it's not actually breaking anything — it's just a noisy log. The real question is whether the **unfiltered** result from cache is causing issues downstream.

**Summary:**

| Environment | Cache State | `caching_request` header | `tryCache` | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| QA (from deprecated `ServiceBasedMapDataProvider`) | Initialized | NOT set | `true` | Tries to compile `hydrate ="Lite"` → fails → logs warning → returns all rules unfiltered from cache |
| Local | Not initialized / exception | N/A | N/A | Falls through to SProc |

**Fix options:**

1. Add the `caching_request` header in `ServiceBasedMapDataProvider` (like the newer `ServiceBasedSnapshotCacheDataProvider` does)
2. Strip non-model properties (like `hydrate`) from the filter before passing it to `_filterTranslator.Parse<Rule>(filter)` in the repository
3. Since `ServiceBasedMapDataProvider` is marked `[Obsolete]`, migrate QA to use `ServiceBasedSnapshotCacheDataProvider` instead

The cleanest fix is option 1 — just add the header in the deprecated provider so it bypasses the cache compilation path, same as the newer provider does.
From where is this -  var cachingRequest = context.RequestHeaders.Get("caching_request"); coming? Is it from browser/UI headers or bff?

It's **not from the browser/UI**. It's a **service-to-service gRPC header** set internally by the cache data providers when they call back into the modeling service to populate the cache.

Look at `ServiceBasedSnapshotCacheDataProvider.cs` line 39:

```csharp
var ruleResponse = await modelingClient.ListModelExpressionRulesAsync(
    new SolvasAm.Common.GrpcApi.V1.ListRequest { Parent = parent, Filter = filter }, 
    headers: new() { { "caching_request", bool.TrueString }},  // <-- set here
    cancellationToken: cancellationToken);
```

This is the **modeling service calling itself** (or another instance of itself) via gRPC to fetch data for its own cache. The `caching_request` header is a custom metadata header injected by the cache data provider to signal: "I'm a cache-building request, don't try to read from the cache to serve me" (which would be circular).

The `ServiceBasedMapDataProvider` (the deprecated one) does the same gRPC call but **forgets to set this header**:

```csharp
var ruleResponse = await modelingClient.ListModelExpressionRulesAsync(
    new SolvasAm.Common.GrpcApi.V1.ListRequest { Parent = parent, Filter = filter }, 
    cancellationToken: cancellationToken);  // <-- no headers
```

So in QA, when the deprecated `ServiceBasedMapDataProvider` is still being used, the request arrives at `ListModelExpressionRules` without the `caching_request` header → `isCachingRequest = false` → `tryCache = true` → it tries to compile `hydrate ="Lite"` as a property filter on `Rule` → fails.

**TL;DR:** It's an internal gRPC header used for self-calls during cache population, not from the browser or BFF.
in QA as well ServiceBasedSnapshotCacheDataProvider

Ah, so QA is also using `ServiceBasedSnapshotCacheDataProvider` which **does** set the `caching_request` header. That means the error isn't coming from the cache data provider call.

Let me re-think this. If the `caching_request` header is set, then `tryCache = false` and the filter compilation path is skipped entirely for that call.

So the question becomes: **who else is calling `GetModelExpressionRulesAsync` with `filter = "hydrate =\"Lite\""` and `tryCache = true`?**

The answer is: **a regular client request** (from BFF/UI). When a user or the BFF calls `ListModelExpressionRules` via gRPC with `filter = "hydrate =\"Lite\""` in the `ListRequest.Filter` field, it arrives at line 1997:

```csharp
var modelItems = await _repository.GetModelExpressionRulesAsync(
    resourceName.OwnerId, request.Filter, context.CancellationToken, !isCachingRequest).ConfigureAwait(false);
```

Since there's no `caching_request` header from the BFF/UI, `tryCache = true`, and the code tries to compile `hydrate ="Lite"` into a `Func<Rule, bool>` — which fails because `hydrate` isn't a property on `Rule`.

**The real issue:** The `hydrate` key is a **meta/control parameter** (it controls serialization depth), not a data filter. But it's being passed in the same `Filter` string field. The repository's cache-filtering logic doesn't know to ignore it.

**The fix should be in the repository** — strip out known non-model filter keys (like `hydrate`) before attempting to compile the predicate. Or better, in the gRPC service layer at line 1997, strip `hydrate` from the filter before passing it to the repository, since it's already extracted separately at line 2000 via `GetHydrateOptionFromFilter`.

Want me to look at how `GetHydrateOptionFromFilter` works and propose a fix that strips `hydrate` from the filter before it reaches the cache predicate compilation?

Good approach. That will confirm whether:

1. The `caching_request` header is actually arriving (or if it's getting stripped/lost in QA's infrastructure — e.g., a load balancer, service mesh, or proxy dropping custom gRPC metadata headers)
2. What the actual value is when it arrives

If you see `caching Request Value: null` or `inside if block of cachingRequest as value is not bool` in QA logs, then the header is being lost in transit — which would explain why QA behaves differently. Common causes:

- **Envoy/sidecar proxy** stripping non-standard headers
- **gRPC load balancer** not forwarding custom metadata
- **Service mesh (e.g., Istio)** filtering headers that aren't in an allow-list

Let me know what the logs show after deployment.

headers: new() { { "caching_request", bool.TrueString }, { "caching-request", bool.TrueString } }

trying to check if it is underscore issue.

Makes sense. gRPC metadata keys are transmitted over HTTP/2, and some proxies/load balancers (especially Envoy, which Istio uses) **reject headers with underscores by default**. Envoy's `headers_with_underscores_action` defaults to dropping them.

So `caching_request` might be silently dropped while `caching-request` (with a hyphen) would pass through fine.

You'll want to update the receiving side too to check both:

```csharp
var cachingRequest = context.RequestHeaders.Get("caching_request") ?? context.RequestHeaders.Get("caching-request");
```

If the hyphen version works in QA, then the permanent fix is to switch to `caching-request` everywhere (both sender and receiver) since hyphens are the standard convention for HTTP headers.
